meta.title: C254 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes GLC C254 engine — M254, OM654, M139. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and hybrid tech explained. Compare prices from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes GLC C254 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned C254 engine typically costs £3,500–£18,000+ supply only, depending on the engine — the volume OM654 diesel and M254 petrol sit at £3,500–£6,000, while the M139-based AMG GLC 63 S unit ranges from £12,000–£18,000+. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes C254 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654.820 diesel (GLC 220d/GLC 300d) and the M254.920 petrol (GLC 200/GLC 300) are the most dependable. The OM654 is a proven, robust diesel introduced in 2016 with a strong reputation for reliability. The M254 is newer but has proven itself in the W206 C-Class. Both feature 48V mild-hybrid assistance, which adds some complexity but is less complex than the PHEV systems.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes GLC C254?

acceptedAnswer.text: Electrical system and software glitches (including a recall for fuse box defects — RC3270), 48V mild-hybrid system issues including ISG and battery failures, PHEV battery degradation on the 25.4 kWh unit, and — for AMG models — the complexity of the electric turbocharger and hybrid system.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes GLC C254 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The C254 is too new for definitive long-term reliability data, but the underlying engine families (OM654, M254, M139) are well-established. Early owner reports indicate significant electrical and software issues on some examples — one owner reported 42 faults in 124 days of ownership. A recall (RC3270) was issued in March 2024 for a fuse box defect that could cause power loss or fire. If buying used, ensure all recalls have been addressed.

mainEntity.name: Which C254 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but the M254-based PHEVs (GLC 300e, GLC 400e) and the GLC 300de PHEV diesel carry the highest long-term risk due to battery degradation and complex hybrid systems. If you're buying used, have the battery health checked by a Mercedes dealer. The AMG GLC 63 S E‑PERFORMANCE is also highly complex and expensive to maintain. Additionally, production of the GLC 43 AMG ends in February 2026, which may affect long-term parts availability.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my C254 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: 48V system warning lights, loss of electric assistance, or ISG failure (all models), reduced electric range or battery warnings (PHEV variants), or check engine lights with drivability issues are key warnings. Also be aware of the March 2024 recall (RC3270) for a fuse box defect that could cause power loss or fire. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes GLC C254 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: The C254 is a current-generation car with strong retained values — a £3,500–£5,500 OM654 recon against a £30,000–£35,000 GLC 220d is 10–16% of the car's value, safely below our 70% Mercedes threshold. Even the M254 at £3,500–£6,000 against a £32,000 GLC 300 is 11–19%. The only exception is the AMG GLC 63 S — but if you can afford a £70,000+ car, you can afford the maintenance. Engine replacement is generally viable across the range, but PHEV battery replacement is a separate, significant cost.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes GLC C254 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 diesel and M254 petrol are expected to exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing, based on their track records in previous models. The M139 in AMG models is typically good for 80,000–120,000 miles if maintained to AMG standards. The PHEV battery will degrade over time — expect 80–100,000 miles before noticeable range reduction, though the 8-year/100,000-mile warranty provides some protection.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the C254 and X254?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both are the second-generation GLC. The C254 is the 5-door coupé (2023–present, this page) with a sloping roofline and more dynamic styling, while the X254 is the 5-door SUV (2022–present) with a more upright, practical body shape. They share the same platform, engines, and most components — but the coupé has a lower roofline and reduced rear headroom. Costs vary slightly by body style — see the X254 generation page for specifics. The coupé is 31 mm longer than the SUV and sits 5 mm higher.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes GLC C254 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the block. Tell us your variant (e.g., GLC 220d, GLC 300, AMG GLC 43) and registration year, and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes GLC C254 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the C254 coupé — from the M254 2.0-litre four-cylinder petrols to the OM654 2.0-litre diesels, the plug-in hybrids with over 74 miles of electric range, and the M139-based AMG GLC 43 and GLC 63 S E-Performance. All C254 engines are electrified — mild hybrid or plug-in hybrid — marking a new era for Mercedes' premium compact SUV coupé. Backed by real UK C254 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes GLC C254 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the C254 coupé specifically — all are four-cylinder units with 48V mild-hybrid or plug-in hybrid assistance, with no purely combustion-engined options available.

engines.power: 204 hp engine + 129 hp motor = 313 hp combined
252 hp engine + 129 hp motor = 381 hp combined
197 hp engine + 129 hp motor = 313 hp combined

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM654 four-cylinder diesel: 80% confidence — introduced in 2016 as Mercedes' first all-aluminium diesel, with a strong reputation for reliability. M254 four-cylinder petrol: 75% confidence — newer engine replacing the M264, with some reported issues being understood. M139 AMG engine: 75% confidence — high-performance unit with electric exhaust gas turbocharger, long-term reliability is still being proven. PHEV variants: 70% confidence — the 25.4 kWh battery and complex hybrid systems are still being proven in the real world. Note that production of the GLC 43 AMG ends in February 2026, with a six-cylinder replacement expected.

overview.intro: The C254 is the coupé body style of the second-generation GLC, launched in March 2023 as the successor to the C253. Based on the MRA2 platform shared with the latest C-Class, it features a longer wheelbase, increased interior space, and a more dynamic exterior design. For the first time, every C254 GLC Coupé is equipped with an electrified powertrain — 48V mild hybrid or plug-in hybrid — with no purely combustion-engined options available. The engine range spans the M254 2.0-litre four-cylinder petrol, the OM654 2.0-litre four-cylinder diesel, and the M139-based AMG units. Plug-in hybrid options (GLC 300e, GLC 400e, GLC 300de, AMG GLC 63 S E‑PERFORMANCE) use a 25.4 kWh battery offering over 74 miles of all-electric range.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes MRA2 • Predecessor: C253 • Successor: — (current generation) • Sibling body style in this generation: X254 SUV.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The C254 GLC Coupé is the current-generation model; enquiry volumes are growing as early 2023 cars exit manufacturer warranty and enter the used market.

items.quote: The 2.0-litre OM654 is a proven, robust diesel with a strong reputation for reliability since its introduction in 2016. The 48V mild-hybrid system adds refinement, and the GLC 300d's 269 hp makes it genuinely quick while maintaining excellent economy. It's the C254's most dependable engine.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want diesel economy and torque without compromising on refinement.

items.quote: The 2.0-litre M254 offers 258 hp with 48V mild-hybrid assistance — it's smooth, refined, and more than enough for UK roads. The M254 is a newer engine but has proven itself in the W206 C-Class. The 23 hp ISG boost provides seamless acceleration.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a modern, efficient petrol without the PHEV's complexity.

items.quote: The M254 four-cylinder petrol is a newer engine, and the complex 25.4 kWh plug-in hybrid system adds significant complexity. The combination of combustion engine, electric motor, and hybrid battery creates more potential failure points than a conventional powertrain. Owner reports indicate electrical issues, software glitches, and self-discharging batteries.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who prioritise low company-car tax and electric commuting over long-term simplicity.

items.quote: The AMG GLC 63 S E‑PERFORMANCE's powertrain is the most complex ever fitted to a GLC — a 2.0-litre M139 engine with an electric exhaust gas turbocharger, a 400-volt high-performance battery with direct cell cooling, and an Electric Drive Unit on the rear axle, producing 680 hp combined. When something goes wrong, it goes wrong expensively. The electric turbocharger, the hybrid system, and the sheer labour intensity of working on this engine mean replacement costs will be eye-watering.
items.whoItsFor: buyers with deep pockets who want the most powerful GLC ever.

items.quote: The GLC 220d is the volume diesel for good reason — 197 hp, 440 Nm of torque, genuine 50+ mpg economy, and the same robust OM654 as the more powerful GLC 300d. It's cheaper to buy, cheaper to run, and cheaper to replace than the petrol alternatives. The sweet spot in the C254 range for most UK buyers.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the best combination of economy, performance, and reliability in the C254 range.

items.quote: The GLC 300de is the most overlooked engine in the C254 range — a 2.0-litre OM654 diesel combined with a 129 hp electric motor and 25.4 kWh battery, delivering 313 hp combined and over 74 miles of electric range. It's the ultimate company car choice — low BIK tax with diesel economy for long journeys.
items.whoItsFor: company car drivers who need the lowest possible tax bills with genuine long-distance capability.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Petrol (Value) — proven M254, mild-hybrid refinement.
AMG Performance — fast, but expensive to maintain.
Most Expensive — the most complex GLC ever.

economicsRule.title: The C254 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,500–£5,500 OM654 recon against a £35,000 C254 sits at 10–16% of the car's value — comfortably safe. Even the M254 at £3,500–£6,000 against a £32,000 GLC 300 is 11–19% — well within our 70% Mercedes threshold. The C254 is a current-generation car with strong retained values; engine replacement is financially viable across the range. The only exception is the AMG GLC 63 S — but if you can afford a £70,000+ car, you can afford the maintenance. For PHEV variants, battery health is the bigger long-term concern.

problems.title: C254 Electrical System & Software Glitches
problems.affectedModels: All C254 variants — particularly early 2023–2024 production.
problems.rootCause: The C254 is heavily software-dependent — owner reports indicate frequent electrical issues including self-discharging batteries, suspension glitches, camera and display failures, and sensor malfunctions. One owner reported 42 faults in 124 days of ownership. A recall campaign (RC3270) was issued in March 2024 for a fuse box defect that could cause power loss or fire.
problems.recommendation: Ensure your C254 has the latest software updates from Mercedes — many electrical issues are resolved with a dealer software update. If you're buying used, check the service history for software update records and whether recall RC3270 has been addressed.

problems.title: C254 48V Mild-Hybrid System Issues
problems.affectedModels: All C254 mild-hybrid variants (GLC 200, GLC 300, GLC 220d, GLC 300d).
problems.rootCause: The 48V electrical system and integrated starter generator (ISG) are complex components — battery degradation, ISG failure, and software glitches have been reported across Mercedes models with this system. The ISG is located between the transmission and the crankshaft.
problems.recommendation: The 48V system is a new technology for the GLC — ensure your car has the latest software updates and have the battery health checked at every service. A £300–£600 check is far cheaper than the £1,800–£3,000 replacement that follows a complete failure.

problems.title: C254 Plug-In Hybrid Battery Degradation
problems.affectedModels: GLC 300e, GLC 400e, GLC 300de, AMG GLC 63 S E‑PERFORMANCE.
problems.rootCause: The 25.4 kWh lithium-ion battery will degrade over time, reducing electric range and potentially requiring replacement. Unlike the MHEV system's small 48V battery, the PHEV battery is large, expensive, and critical to vehicle operation.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a used C254 PHEV, have the battery health checked by a Mercedes dealer — battery degradation is the single biggest long-term cost risk. Mercedes offers an 8-year/100,000-mile battery warranty, but check the terms carefully.

cta.label: Compare C254 PHEV battery health checks →

problems.title: AMG GLC 43 Production Ending (2026)
problems.rootCause: Production of the GLC 43 AMG as both SUV (X254) and Coupé (C254) ends in February 2026, to be replaced by a six-cylinder engine. This means parts availability and specialist knowledge for the four-cylinder M139 may become more limited over time.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying an AMG GLC 43, be aware that production ends in February 2026. While parts will remain available, specialist knowledge for this engine may become harder to find. Consider this in your long-term ownership planning.

replacementCosts.h2: C254 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — C254 coupé shares its MRA2-derived engine bay with the X254 SUV, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required. M139 AMG costs reflect AMG-specific parts, the electric exhaust gas turbocharger, hybrid system components, and specialist labour requirements.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are C254 coupé only. X254 SUV shares most of these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page, as fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style. The C254's coupé roofline and sloping tailgate give it a more dynamic appearance than the upright GLC SUV.
